source: MDC Google Drive document / FULL GAME SCRIPT tab

R.strong: You named a specific contribution before raising the gap, helping the employee feel seen rather than ambushed.
R.developing: You kept the opening kind, but recognition works best when it names a specific behaviour or contribution.
R.follow_up: Recognise needs attention. Start with observable evidence of what the employee did well before discussing the concern.

E.strong: You balanced positive contribution with evidence and accountability instead of rating the person by likeability.
E.developing: You considered both sides, but the evaluation could be more clearly anchored to evidence and standards.
E.follow_up: Evaluate needs attention. Weigh observable outcomes and behaviours, not feelings or personality labels.

A.strong: You offered a practical, manageable path forward and treated advice as navigation rather than a command.
A.developing: Your response moved toward support, but the next action could be more specific or collaborative.
A.follow_up: Advise needs attention. Ask first, then agree on a small and concrete action the employee can practise.

L.strong: You connected the present behaviour to future growth and wider team impact, giving the feedback meaning.
L.developing: You hinted at why the feedback matters, but the future benefit could be made more visible.
L.follow_up: Link needs attention. Explain how today's behaviour shapes future growth, purpose, or team outcomes.

C.strong: You paused, regulated the first reaction, and stayed present long enough to hear the feedback.
C.developing: You stayed outwardly calm, but composing also means remaining present instead of escaping the message.
C.follow_up: Compose needs attention. Pause and breathe before responding so emotion does not bury the feedback.

A.strong: You separated the emotional reaction from the evidence and asked for the criteria behind the message.
A.developing: You looked for clarity, but could focus more directly on evidence rather than assumed intent.
A.follow_up: Analyze needs attention. Examine what was said and ask for evidence before deciding what it means.

R.strong: You clarified the feedback privately and respectfully, turning a vague message into useful understanding.
R.developing: You asked for clarity, but the timing or delivery introduced avoidable tension.
R.follow_up: Resolve needs attention. Ask specific questions respectfully instead of assuming or staying silent.

E.strong: You converted feedback into small, trackable actions and built in a follow-up point.
E.developing: You took action, but the plan could be more specific, sustainable, or measurable.
E.follow_up: Execute needs attention. Break the feedback into practical steps, a timeline, and a follow-up.

reflectionPrompts.label: What made your response effective or ineffective?
What would you do in the next conversation?
What part of the feedback became clearer?

scenarios.title: The Late Arrival
scenarios.description: Recognise a strong contribution before discussing a repeated punctuality concern.

profile.name: Jamie
profile.headline: Strong report / Repeated lateness
profile.text: Jamie has arrived 15-20 minutes late almost every day this week. Today, Jamie also submitted the strongest community outreach report the team has seen in months.
profile.displayText: Jamie produced an excellent report, but has been repeatedly late this week.

prompt.speaker: Scene
prompt.text: You sit down with Jamie to discuss both the excellent outreach report and the repeated lateness. Jamie asks whether everything is okay. What do you open with?
prompt.displayText: Jamie delivered excellent work and has also been late all week. How will you open?

choices.text: Recognise the report specifically, then raise the lateness

response.speaker: Alex
response.text: Your report was genuinely impressive, especially how you broke down the volunteer data. I want us to use that format again. Because I value that work, I also want to understand the lateness I have noticed this week.
response.displayText: Your report was genuinely impressive. I also want to understand the lateness I noticed this week.

feedback.text: You demonstrated Recognise. Specific praise created psychological safety before the concern was raised.
feedback.displayText: Specific recognition helps Jamie feel seen, not ambushed.

choices.text: Lead immediately with the repeated lateness

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: I know I have been late, but I have had transport problems and I am still getting my work done.
response.displayText: I know I was late, but I still delivered the work.

feedback.text: You skipped Recognise. Jamie leaves knowing only what went wrong, while the excellent report goes unseen.
feedback.displayText: The concern lands as judgement because the contribution was ignored.

choices.text: Begin with a general workload check-in

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: I am okay, just a little stretched. I will try to do better with the punctuality.
response.displayText: I am a little stretched. I will try to do better.

feedback.text: The opening was kind but vague. Recognition is stronger when it names a real contribution before the gap.
feedback.displayText: Kindness helped, but vague recognition did not make Jamie feel seen.

scenarios.title: The Uneven Scale
scenarios.description: Evaluate a popular high performer using evidence rather than enthusiasm or labels.

profile.name: Jordan
profile.headline: Relief-drive leader / Two missed deadlines
profile.text: Jordan is energetic and deeply valued by the team. Jordan went above and beyond at the relief drive, but missed two deadlines that affected the planning cycle.
profile.displayText: Jordan inspired the relief drive but missed two deadlines that affected planning.

prompt.speaker: Scene
prompt.text: Jordan acknowledges getting caught up in the relief drive. You now need to decide how the missed deadlines should affect the review.
prompt.displayText: Jordan delivered exceptional energy and missed two deadlines. How will you evaluate both?

choices.text: Set the deadlines aside because the relief-drive impact was worth it

response.speaker: Outcome
response.text: The review becomes almost entirely positive. When Sam hears about it, the team's sense of fairness quietly shifts.
response.displayText: The near-perfect review weakens the team's sense of fairness.

feedback.text: You skipped Evaluate. Enthusiasm cannot replace evidence, and fairness matters to both Jordan and the wider team.
feedback.displayText: Evaluate contributions and missed outcomes together.

choices.text: Recognise the energy and evaluate the missed deadlines too

response.speaker: Jordan
response.text: That is fair. I did not think about how the missed deadlines affected the planning team downstream.
response.displayText: That is fair. I did not see the downstream planning impact.

feedback.text: You demonstrated Evaluate by balancing recognition with accountability and observable outcomes.
feedback.displayText: Evidence keeps the review honest, balanced, and fair.

choices.text: Label Jordan as a people person who is weak at administration

response.speaker: Jordan
response.text: I did not realise you saw me as someone who could not handle the full role.
response.displayText: I did not realise you saw me as incapable of the full role.

feedback.text: A personality label activated an identity trigger. Evaluate what happened, not who you think the person is.
feedback.displayText: Assess behaviour and outcomes, not identity.

scenarios.title: The Quiet One
scenarios.description: Turn a participation gap into a practical and supportive next step.

profile.name: Priya
profile.headline: Strong individual work / Quiet in meetings
profile.text: Priya is a newer staff member who produces strong individual work but rarely speaks in team meetings. Priya worries that her ideas are not polished enough.
profile.displayText: Priya produces strong work but disappears in group discussions.

prompt.speaker: Priya
prompt.text: I do not always feel like I have something worth saying in meetings.
prompt.displayText: I do not always feel like my ideas are worth sharing.

choices.text: Tell Priya to speak up because others notice the silence

response.speaker: Priya
response.text: I will try.
response.displayText: I will try.

feedback.text: You named the gap but offered no path forward. Pressure without guidance increases anxiety.
feedback.displayText: Direction without a practical path is pressure, not advice.

choices.text: Ask what makes it difficult to share in those moments

response.speaker: Priya
response.text: I worry my ideas are not as polished as everyone else's. A heads-up on one agenda item would help.
response.displayText: I worry my ideas are not polished. A heads-up would help.

feedback.text: You asked before advising and uncovered a practical, low-pressure next step.
feedback.displayText: Reflective questions make advice collaborative and useful.

choices.text: Suggest preparing one idea and promise to create space

response.speaker: Priya
response.text: That actually sounds manageable. Let us try it for two weeks.
response.displayText: That sounds manageable. I can try that.

feedback.text: A small, specific action respected Priya's pace and made the path forward concrete.
feedback.displayText: Small and specific steps turn advice into coaching.

scenarios.title: The Star Who Stopped Caring
scenarios.description: Reconnect a high performer with purpose and future leadership impact.

profile.name: Marcus
profile.headline: Top performer / Disengaged from mentoring
profile.text: Marcus has the team's strongest results but has become quiet in planning sessions and declined an opportunity to mentor a junior officer.
profile.displayText: Marcus delivers the best results, but no longer sees the point of mentoring.

prompt.speaker: Marcus
prompt.text: I stepped back from mentoring because I just did not see the point.
prompt.displayText: I did not see the point of mentoring someone.

choices.text: Say mentoring is expected at Marcus's level

response.speaker: Marcus
response.text: Okay. If it is expected, I will do it.
response.displayText: Okay. I will do it because it is expected.

feedback.text: Compliance without meaning does not last. Marcus needs to understand why the action matters.
feedback.displayText: Expectation alone creates compliance, not purpose.

choices.text: Connect mentoring to Marcus's future leadership impact

response.speaker: Marcus
response.text: I had not thought about mentoring as shaping the next generation of the team.
response.displayText: I had not thought about mentoring as a leadership opportunity.

feedback.text: You demonstrated Link by connecting today's choice to Marcus's future potential and the team's mission.
feedback.displayText: Meaning reconnects Marcus with growth and wider impact.

choices.text: Accept the decision and move on to next year's targets

response.speaker: Outcome
response.text: The moment passes. Marcus leaves the review feeling exactly as disconnected as before.
response.displayText: The moment passes and Marcus remains disconnected.

feedback.text: Feedback that stops at information does not move people. Link it to future growth and impact.
feedback.displayText: Without a future connection, the feedback does not stick.

scenarios.title: The Ambush
scenarios.description: Regulate an exhausted first reaction and stay present for difficult feedback.

profile.name: Jamie
profile.headline: Exhausted / Feedback arrives unexpectedly
profile.text: After a long understaffed week and a difficult volunteer event, Jamie is stopped in the corridor and told that teammates feel dismissed in morning briefings.
profile.displayText: You are exhausted when Alex raises unexpected feedback in the corridor.

prompt.speaker: Alex
prompt.text: Jamie, a few team members feel their ideas are not being heard in the morning briefings.
prompt.displayText: Some teammates feel their ideas are not being heard.

choices.text: Defend how much you have carried this week

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: Are you serious? I have been the only one holding everything together this week.
response.displayText: Are you serious? I have been holding everything together.

feedback.text: Exhaustion hijacked the conversation and buried the feedback under the emotional reaction.
feedback.displayText: Reacting immediately turns the conversation into a defence.

choices.text: Ask for a moment so you can hear the feedback properly

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: Can I have a moment? I am tired and want to make sure I hear this properly. Then please walk me through what was shared.
response.displayText: Can I pause for a moment? I want to hear this properly.

feedback.text: You demonstrated Compose by pausing, reframing internally, and returning with calm curiosity.
feedback.displayText: The pause keeps emotion from controlling the response.

choices.text: Say you will fix it and leave immediately

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: That is fine. I will fix it.
response.displayText: That is fine. I will fix it.

feedback.text: You stayed calm but escaped the message. Compose also means staying present enough to understand.
feedback.displayText: Calm avoidance is still avoidance.

scenarios.title: The Rating That Stings
scenarios.description: Separate disappointment from evidence when a performance rating feels unfair.

profile.name: Jamie
profile.headline: Expected Exceeds / Rated Meets
profile.text: Jamie covered shifts, worked late, and delivered every project. The mid-year rating is Meets Expectations, although Jamie expected Exceeds.
profile.displayText: You expected Exceeds, but Alex gives you a Meets Expectations rating.

prompt.speaker: Alex
prompt.text: Overall, your performance this cycle is solid. The rating is Meets Expectations.
prompt.displayText: Your performance is solid. The rating is Meets Expectations.

choices.text: Ask Alex to walk through the rating criteria

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: I am confused because I felt I exceeded expectations in some areas. Can you walk me through the criteria and the breakdown?
response.displayText: Can you walk me through the criteria and evidence?

feedback.text: You demonstrated Analyze by asking for evidence rather than validation or assumed intent.
feedback.displayText: Evidence replaces uncertainty with useful clarity.

choices.text: Say the rating feels unfair and personal

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: I think this rating is unfair. It feels personal.
response.displayText: This rating feels unfair and personal.

feedback.text: The conversation shifts to assumed motivation instead of facts, so no clarity is reached.
feedback.displayText: Assumed intent blocks the evidence you need.

choices.text: Accept the rating silently and disengage

response.speaker: Outcome
response.text: The frustration stays with you for weeks, while the performance gap remains invisible.
response.displayText: You leave frustrated and still do not understand the gap.

feedback.text: Silence is avoidance, not analysis. Examine the content and remain curious.
feedback.displayText: Without analysis, the feedback cannot support growth.

scenarios.title: What Did That Mean?
scenarios.description: Clarify vague group feedback privately and respectfully.

profile.name: Jamie
profile.headline: Vague group feedback / Possible personal impact
profile.text: After a community health event, Alex says the team's communication and coordination need improvement. Jamie managed volunteer communications but does not know what went wrong.
profile.displayText: Alex gives vague group feedback that may refer to your volunteer communications.

prompt.speaker: Alex
prompt.text: Some communications during the event could have been clearer. We need to coordinate better next time.
prompt.displayText: Some event communications were unclear. We need better coordination next time.

choices.text: Assume it was about you and decide to work harder

response.speaker: Outcome
response.text: You replay the event at home but still cannot identify what needs to change. The same issue returns next time.
response.displayText: You work harder without knowing what actually needs to change.

feedback.text: You skipped Resolve. Assuming without asking risks fixing the wrong thing or nothing at all.
feedback.displayText: You cannot improve feedback you do not understand.

choices.text: Ask Alex privately for a specific example

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: Before we wrap up, can we check in privately? I want to understand the communications feedback and what happened.
response.displayText: Can we speak privately so I can understand the specific issue?

feedback.text: You demonstrated Resolve. The private question reveals that late check-in messages sent volunteers to the wrong station.
feedback.displayText: A respectful question turns vague feedback into a clear action.

choices.text: Challenge the feedback publicly and defend your role

response.speaker: Alex
response.text: It was not only about you, Jamie. Let us discuss the details separately.
response.displayText: It was not only about you. Let us discuss it separately.

feedback.text: You asked for clarity, but public defensiveness created tension. The what and the how both count.
feedback.displayText: The question was useful; the public delivery was not.

scenarios.title: Three Weeks. One Goal.
scenarios.description: Convert clear writing feedback into small, trackable improvement steps.

profile.name: Jamie
profile.headline: Detailed reports / Difficult to skim
profile.detail: Build the habit, not a quick fix
profile.text: Alex has explained that Jamie's reports are detailed but hard to skim. A new report is due in three weeks.
profile.displayText: Your reports are detailed but difficult to skim. The next one is due in three weeks.

prompt.speaker: Scene
prompt.text: The feedback is fair. You have three weeks before the next report is due. What do you do?
prompt.displayText: You have three weeks to act on the writing feedback. What is your plan?

choices.text: Overhaul your entire writing style in the first week

response.speaker: Outcome
response.text: You test several formats and the report feels forced. Alex sees improvement, but the change is not yet sustainable.
response.displayText: Changing everything at once feels forced and does not stick.

feedback.text: Big leaps without structure rarely last. Execute through small, specific, trackable steps.
feedback.displayText: A complete overhaul is too broad to become a habit.

choices.text: Request an example, choose two changes, and schedule a check-in

response.speaker: Jamie
response.text: I will lead each section with the main finding, shorten the executive summary to three lines, and check the draft with Alex in week two.
response.displayText: I will make two specific changes and review the draft in week two.

feedback.text: You demonstrated Execute by using a reference, manageable actions, and a progress check before the deadline.
feedback.displayText: Small steps and follow-up turn feedback into real growth.

choices.text: Wait and adapt the report on the fly

response.speaker: Outcome
response.text: The report is acceptable, but the same feedback returns three months later because no lasting habit was built.
response.displayText: The report passes, but the same feedback returns later.

feedback.text: Adapting once is survival, not execution. Plan ahead and make the improvement part of how you work.
feedback.displayText: A one-time adjustment does not create lasting change.
